sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Auscultation is the process of listening to sounds produced by the body, most commonly the lungs and heart, using a stethoscope. In the prehospital setting it is used to assess ventilation, identify abnormal breath sounds, confirm endotracheal tube placement, and assess cardiac rhythm.

sections.title: Technique
sections.content:
**Stethoscope**
- Use the diaphragm (flat side) for high-pitched sounds: breath sounds, bowel sounds, normal heart sounds
- Use the bell (concave side) for low-pitched sounds: heart murmurs, extra heart sounds
- Ensure earpieces are angled forward to match ear canal direction
- Eliminate background noise where possible

**Lung Auscultation**
1. Expose the chest; ask patient to breathe deeply through mouth
2. Auscultate bilaterally — at least 2 sites per side anteriorly (apices, bases)
3. Compare left and right sides at each level
4. Posterior auscultation preferred when accessible

**Breath Sound Landmarks (Anterior)**
- Upper lobes: 2nd intercostal space, mid-clavicular line
- Lower lobes: 4th–5th intercostal space, anterior axillary line

**Heart Auscultation**
- Aortic: 2nd ICS, right sternal border
- Pulmonary: 2nd ICS, left sternal border
- Tricuspid: 4th ICS, left sternal border
- Mitral (Apex): 5th ICS, mid-clavicular line

sections.title: Interpretation of Findings
sections.content:
**Normal Breath Sounds:**
- Vesicular: soft, rustling, heard over most lung fields
- Bronchial: loud, hollow, heard over trachea and mainstem bronchi
- Bronchovesicular: intermediate, heard over main bronchi

**Abnormal Breath Sounds:**
- **Absent / reduced**: pneumothorax, haemothorax, pleural effusion, mucous plug, endobronchial intubation
- **Crackles (rales)**: fine = pulmonary oedema, pneumonia; coarse = secretions, bronchitis
- **Wheezes (rhonchi)**: bronchospasm (asthma, COPD, anaphylaxis) — high-pitched; secretions — low-pitched
- **Stridor**: upper airway obstruction — inspiratory, high-pitched
- **Pleural rub**: pleurisy — leathery, scratching sound

**Confirming ETT Placement:**
- Auscultate epigastrium first (absent gurgling)
- Then bilateral chest (equal breath sounds)
- Combine with waveform capnography for definitive confirmation

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Prehospital environments are noisy — use clinical context alongside auscultation findings
- Unilateral absent breath sounds after trauma = pneumothorax until proven otherwise
- Always auscultate before and after any airway intervention
- Document findings as bilateral equal air entry, or describe specific abnormalities
